topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom was a shimmering expanse of silk and forced merriment. I was nursing a lukewarm champagne, trying to appear engaged in a conversation with a distant cousin, when a familiar silhouette caught my eye across the crowded floor. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that stole the oxygen from my lungs. It was him. Liam. He was standing near the buffet, laughing with someone I vaguely recognized, and the world seemed to tilt on its axis. My hand, gripping the glass, began to tremble, and I felt a distinct pressure building behind my sternum. I forced myself to look away, focusing intently on the intricate pattern of the wallpaper, but every nerve ending in my body was screaming. When he finally turned, his eyes swept the room, and for a split second, they locked onto mine. A slow, almost imperceptible widening of his gaze sent a jolt right through me. I couldn't speak; I could only stare, my mouth suddenly dry, a strange, hollow feeling settling in my chest.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who it is!" Sarah’s voice cut through the din, and I turned, bracing myself for a friendly greeting. But the warmth drained from my face as I saw him standing just beyond the cluster of guests—Mark. He was impeccably dressed, radiating an easy confidence that used to pull me in, and now it felt like a physical blow. My carefully constructed composure fractured. I stopped mid-sip of my drink, the liquid sloshing precariously in the glass. My feet felt suddenly rooted to the polished marble floor. I watched as he approached, and the air around him seemed to thicken. When he finally reached me, his smile was practiced, but the intensity in his eyes made my stomach clench. I managed a small, jerky nod, my throat suddenly constricted, unable to form a coherent response. I just stood there, my gaze snagged on his face, unable to look away, feeling a profound disorientation wash over me.

[story 3]

I was trying to navigate the throng near the dance floor, feeling slightly overwhelmed by the sheer volume of people, when I spotted him. He was leaning against a pillar, talking animatedly with a group of older relatives. The sound of his laughter, familiar and rich, hit me like a physical wave. Everything else—the music, the clinking glasses, the chatter—receded into a dull, indistinct hum. My focus narrowed entirely onto him. My palms started sweating, and I had to consciously clench my jaw to keep my expression neutral. When he finally glanced over, his eyes met mine across the distance. There was a flicker in his expression, a momentary pause in his conversation, and then he gave a small, almost hesitant nod. A strange, dizzying sensation washed over me, like the floor had momentarily disappeared beneath my feet. I took an involuntary step backward, suddenly needing space, my heart hammering against my ribs with an alarming, erratic rhythm.
